statement.markdown: Given an array of integers representing the heights of a histogram, find the maximum area of a rectangle that can be formed using the histogram bars. The area of a rectangle is calculated as the product of its width and height.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n, representing the number of bars in the histogram. The second line contains n space-separated integers representing the heights of the bars.
statement.outputFormat: The maximum area of a rectangle that can be formed using the histogram bars.

constraints.description: Number of bars in the histogram

examples.explanation: The above histogram has 5 bars of heights 2, 1, 5, 6, and 2. The maximum area of a rectangle that can be formed is 10 (using the bars with heights 5 and 6).
The maximum area of a rectangle that can be formed is 6 (using the bars with heights 3 and 4).

hints.text: Consider the problem as finding the maximum sum of a subarray in an array of heights.
Use dynamic programming to store the maximum area for subarrays and update it when a higher height is found.
Initialize an array of size n (number of bars) with zeros and fill it using the maximum sum of subarrays.
At each index i, consider the maximum area by summing the heights of the subarray from i to the end (n-1).
The maximum area is stored in the last element of the filled array.

editorial.title: Unlocking the Optimal Approach to Maximum Rectangles
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the maximum area of rectangles that can be formed within a histogram. A brute force approach would involve checking all possible rectangles, but this is inefficient. A key observation is that a larger rectangle can be formed by extending a smaller rectangle to the right as long as the height of the bars allows it.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve iterating over all pairs of bars and calculating the area of the rectangle that can be formed between them. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2) or worse, making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a stack to keep track of indices of bars. It iterates through the bars, popping from the stack when a bar of smaller height is encountered, and calculates the area of the rectangle with the popped bar as the smallest bar.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(n), making it much more efficient than the brute force approach.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it considers all possible rectangles and calculates their areas correctly. When a bar is popped from the stack, it is used as the smallest bar to calculate the area of the rectangle. The width of the rectangle is calculated as the difference between the current index and the index of the previous bar in the stack (or the start of the histogram if the stack is empty). This ensures that all possible rectangles are considered.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with a sample input helps verify its correctness. For example, with the input [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3], the stack is used to keep track of indices, and the maximum area is calculated correctly.
commonMistakes: Not considering the case when the stack is empty when calculating the width of a rectangle.
Not popping all bars from the stack after iterating through all bars.
Incorrectly calculating the width of a rectangle.
Not updating the maximum area correctly.
Not handling the case when the current bar is equal to the bar at the top of the stack.
